sentences.text: That was the first major thing what I noticed.
Of course, like I said, the Germans were there.
And once in a while, I don't know where they went to, but there were big convoys going through, you know, the army convoys on-- on the main road.
But this was only real sign of the war, but there were other things too.
There were the nightly air raids, which started to get worse in '41, '42.
The British used to bomb at night, and the Americans by day.
I knew that even then.
You know.
The British liked to fly at night.
The Americans didn't like to fly at night.

This I didn't know at the time, but I knew the British flew at night and the Americans by day because-- I found this out because the planes got shot down, you know, and that's how I found out who-- who was who.
Our little village, Dinxperlo, was right on the border.
It was almost cut in half by big barbed wire.
On the other side was a German-- little German village, and it was the beginning of the [? rural ?] area, OK?
Heavy industrialized area, which in '41, '42 was a main target of the Allies.
And apparently, the way it used to-- they-- they used to sort of assemble above us.
They used to come from England, the airplanes, and used to assemble in-- not just above us, but in a general area and then continue.
They came from three different directions apparently, and at the same time, this also was-- the Germans came from the-- from-- from their own air fields and tried to shoot the planes down.
Plus, there was anti-aircraft guns in the area too.
Heavy, very heavy.
There was-- the post-- the-- one of the big buildings in the village was a post office.
On top of the post office was the-- this air raid siren, and the air raid siren used to take their cue, I think, from-- from Germany.
They used to hear the sirens in Germany so they [? knew ?] [? system. ?] We used to hear them also in the distance, and then-- then that siren went off.
And especially at night, it-- if it wasn't so dangerous, it would be like a sound and light show because you saw the-- the-- what do you call them.
The lights-- you know, the-- what do you call the lights which came from the ground into the air?
Search lights.
Search lights, right.
Right.
So the search lights, big search lights, tried to pick out the planes, OK, in the sky, and then they would shoot-- try to shoot them from the ground.
You know.
And then the sky was crisscrossed with search lights, OK?
And then you saw also some battles, you know, at night, and shooting.
You could see that because apparently these were like trace-- like tracer bullets which left signs.
And then, in time, airplanes used to crash in our area quite a lot.
I mean, there were some nights when there were very heavy air raids.
Again, this is something which I didn't know at the time.
There was, like, 1,000 planes at the time when they-- when they attacked Cologne.
I-- I remember counting one night 15, 16 planes which crashed in our-- in our general area, and a lot of bombs fell.
Why did the bombs fall?
They didn't bomb us really, but if a plane was hit, they used to jettison the bombs in order to make it lighter.
Maybe they could make it back to-- to England, so they jettisoned, where-- wherever they could.
So they used to fall on our village, or the next village, or in-- or Grossenbrode in Germany.
And luckily, like I said, the houses were sort of spaced out.
It wasn't-- it wasn't a heavily built area, so a lot of the bombs used to fall into open fields.
Some, of course, also fell on houses, and it came to that kind of situation where almost every night, we were up.
You know.
At the beginning, I used to sleep.
I used to hear it in my sleep.
It was-- the bombing was so heavy, I used to hear it in my sleep.
Everything used to shake, but I used to sleep though.
You know.
And then, my mom-- my sister used to wake me up.
She was afraid and used to wake me up, and we used to sit downstairs and-- and-- to wait for whatever.
So that's when I used to start going out and see what was happening.
But to go back to the school part, they started-- the law came that we had to-- no longer could go to school after a certain date.
So I remember the last day in school, you know, I remember the teacher being very unhappy about it, the Dutch teacher.
And he said, I'm sorry that you have to go-- leave.
You always were good students, and-- and I hope you-- everything is going to be OK.
And then they had all-- there was a Jewish school which was organized in a town which was about 15 kilometers away called [?
Dueterhum. ?] It was a bigger town where all the little villages-- a lot of little villages in the area, and they all had a Jewish population.
And we all went to this school in [?
Dueterhum. ?]
How did we go?
The first five or six kilometer were by horse and cart, because a bus didn't come into our village anymore.
And then we took this-- then we went to what was called the main road.
Then we took one of those buses, which I explained before with those smoking things at the back and then took it to [?
Dueterhum. ?]
And that was-- it was a whole day thing.
By the time we got back, it was in the evening.
The horse and cart waited for us at-- at the-- in a-- in a town called-- it wasn't a town, it was like a spot, called [?
Bontebok. ?] That-- it still exists.
You know.
And that's like a crossroads, and that's where they were used to wait for us-- the horse and cart-- and take us back to home.
It was about six or eight kids who went on that little horse and cart.
Then also, we had to start wearing the Jewish star in 1942.
I thought around June, July '42, I think it was.
This was mandatory.
OK.
If you-- the threat was if they caught you without a Jewish star, you were immediately taken to a concentration camp, to a labor camp.
It was in Dutch.
Jood was written on it.
Jood means Jew.
It was six-- you know, the Jewish star, six-- in yellow-- yellow with black letters.
Yes.
I didn't really-- forgot to tell you.
One of the things which the Germans did at the very beginning after the invasion of Holland was-- looked very innocent.
They made everybody register, have an identity card, OK?
Now this is a very innocent thing, but what-- what did they do if you were Jewish, you had to put-- they put a big J on your identity card.
And of course, the Dutch Civil Service, which continued to function, did not-- did not resign-- maybe some people resigned.
The Jews, of course, were thrown out.
But this Dutch Civil Service continued to function under the direction of the Germans, you know, as if nothing had happened, and they give out identity cards.
And-- and some people realized-- well, maybe people didn't even realize at the time what it meant, you know.
But right away you were separated because you were the only one who had a different identity card.
This is how they separated you, OK?

And this was done, very easy for them, you know, because at the beginning, I guess, except for very few-- there might have been a few people who were smart who managed to maybe find a way of not getting the identity card, but I've never come across anybody but there could have been.
But I imagine most people didn't.
Especially, as you went to your local registrar who knew you, so he wasn't gonna put-- if you were Jewish, he wasn't going to say no.
They just functioned under the orders of the Germans as if nothing had happened.
So this is how they could identify you.
And they-- if they had suspicion, they would ask for your identity card, and-- and if you had Jew on it and you didn't wear the star, and it was just too bad for you.
So that's how they-- that was the next step.
Now you said that they were-- threatened to take you to the camps.
What-- what were you hearing about camps?
It's very strange what I heard, because the only camp I heard of-- and this was-- the threat, was a camp called Mauthausen.
It was in Austria.
Apparently-- how did I hear about it?
Because apparently in Amsterdam, they had rounded up in the ghetto that there was-- the Jews were ghettoized in Amsterdam.
There was a general strike in 1941 over about three days of the dockworkers, and-- and some working-- you know, workers strike, and there was a sort of riot in the ghetto.
You know, the-- the Dutch Nazi-- you know, the Dutch police and the Nazis went into the ghetto and started beating up the Jews, so the ghetto organized.
And some of the men had started beating up the Dutch Nazis, and they chased them out of the ghetto, out of the streets.
It wasn't the ghetto like it was in Poland.
It was sort of certain streets, OK?
Well, they chased them out of there, so then the German police had to come and-- to the aid of the-- the Dutch Nazis.
So they, in order to-- as a warning, they picked up, I don't know, a couple of hundred-- I think 200 young Dutch Jews and sent them to Mauthausen, and they were killed in Mauthausen.
I think that's how I knew about Mauthausen, because word got around, you know, what-- what-- what was happening.
And also, when I started going to the school, at the beginning there were about 80 kids there.
Might have been a few less, but between 60 and 80 kids.
And every week there were less, OK?
Now, there were two reasons for this.
One reason, some parents did go into hiding with their children, and some got picked up, you know, by the Germans.
The Germans used to come at night.
In the bigger cities, they had to-- they called them razzias.
They used to sort of cordon off a certain area where they knew many Jews lived, at night, like close it off, and then go from apartment to apartment or house to house.
And-- and whoever was Jewish was taken out and put in-- in a holding area, and then taken to Westerbork which was a transit camp.
And I knew about this, and I heard about this.
Also, on another occasion, this must have been, I think, on the high holidays, 1942.
I don't think it was 1940.
It must have been 1942.
It was not-- not long before we went.
Dinxperlo was a very-- it was a small village, but it had a well-established Jewish community.
Small, but well established.
There were some wealthy Jews living there.
There were, for instance, two brothers called Prince who had a carpet factory where half the village worked there.
And they had huge houses, and I guess they supported the synagogue.
They supported the community.

And I remember that one of the Princes lived opposite us, had a big-- like a big yard where everybody-- all the kids used to play-- Jews or non-Jews used to play there, used to play football, soccer, you know, like you're playing. and we were friendly with them, you know.
And we went to services on Erev Yom Kippur and [INAUDIBLE].
And I would say there may have been in-- in those days, I think, only men-- only the men went to-- to the synagogue, at least on [INAUDIBLE].
And I don't know many people were in the synagogue.
I don't remember.
There must have been at least 30, 40.
Maybe less-- you know, 20, whatever.
But at-- at least a good amount of people, OK?
And the rumor spread that the Germans were coming to pick up the men and take them to Mauthausen.
That's-- that's how I always-- Mauthausen always reminded me-- me of my-- my memory from those days, you know, for two reasons.
One of the reasons, I heard about it before, and it was frightening.
You know.
This was the threat, that everybody would be sent to Mauthausen.
So the rumor was that on Yom Kippur evening they were going to be picked up.
And the rumor and the message got to the synagogue.
And it was evening, so the men one by one started to leave.
You know, they didn't want to be caught in the synagogue, you know.
So they left one by one.
The only people left in the end was the-- the hazzan, you knwo, the cantor, and my cousin and myself, OK?
And then the cantor asks, why-- why-- why don't you leave?
I mean, what are you doing here?
You know, you better go home.
So we left, and we went home.
But the threat at the time was that we'd be picked up and-- and sent to Mauthausen.
You know.
And it could have been that the rumor came-- often the Germans planted rumors, you know, in order to, you know, frighten people and then do whatever they wanted to do,
I don't know whether it was a real threat, or whether it wasn't, but I know that's the last time I was in a synagogue.
I don't think anybody went to the synagogue anymore after that, because very soon afterwards, the-- there was a law that by-- by a certain time, we had-- the Jews had to leave the provinces.
You no longer were allowed to live outside of the big cities.
The provinces were anything outside Amsterdam or The Hague.
You know, Holland is 11 provinces, you know, that's-- that's-- and we we not allowed to live there anymore.
Now this was the end of '42 beginning of '43 when this came, OK?
And-- so my uncle, who had a big problem because he had to take care-- asides from his own family, he had to take care of two of us.
Then he had this mother-in-law from his first wife living with him, OK?
That's my-- my older cousin's grandmother.
Her name was Maita Woresack, and she was living there also.
Then there were three aunts also who were related to his-- not-- they were-- were not my aunts.
I don't even remember their name, but they were living nearby.
They were related to-- to-- to him through his first wife, and he was taking care of them too.
Basically, he was paying for-- then for them to live with neighbors, but their food-- my aunt had to cook it for them.
And I remember I used to take-- that-- that's what I knew about that.
I used to take them their food.
In-- in-- in the afternoon, at night, I used to bring their food to them, hot food.
You know.
And the only thing I remember about them before-- when this situation arose, they-- they gave you time.
It wasn't from today till tomorrow.
By a certain date, we had to-- the provinces had to be what they called judenrein, clean of Jews.
I'm sure you've heard the word before.
In the meanwhile, those three aunts-- it was winter.
They had the-- they had some sort of fire burning, and they got some of-- some sort of carbon monoxide poisoning.
That I remember.
They were taken to hospital, all three of them, OK?
They didn't die at the time, but they all were taken to a hospital.
That I remember.
I don't know what happened to them afterwards.
I know they didn't survive, but whether they were taken from the hospital to-- to a concentration camp, whether they died there, I don't know, OK?
But we, in 1943, went-- were taken to a camp called Vught, which was a concentration camp.
So when they said you had to move--
Yeah.
No, no, no.
They told us where to move to, OK?
I mean, there was two possibilities-- or to go into hiding, which some people did-- or to go where they told you to go.
You know.
Now, the Germans, the difference between Dinxperlo and Amsterdam was in Amsterdam or The Hague or other big cities, they physically came to your door and dragged you out or took you out.
Sometimes if they were very civil, they let you-- they give you an hour to pack your things and then took you out.
In-- in little villages like that, they didn't bother, you know, because it was too much.
So they told you by certain hour, you have to be there, and then you go from there to there.
So people complied and some of the people didn't comply.
But they didn't stay.
They-- they went into hiding, some people.
My uncle could have gone into hiding.
He had the opportunity through my-- through his brothers, through my-- through Adolf, I'm sure.
I know that-- I know that for a fact that he had been in touch and had offered him a possibility, but I think he was afraid.
His nerves-- I think his nerves were shot already.
He was taking care of everybody, and I'm sure he must have had huge money problems, which I, of course, as a little child, didn't realize to take care of everybody but he did.
So and then of course, that-- that-- the nightly air raids didn't add to-- to-- to the-- to his comfort.
You know.
And everything was on his shoulders, so I don't think he had the nerves to-- to-- to go into hiding.
Because you need those very strong nerves for that, too.
You know, when you went into hiding, you went to live in somebody's house usually.
You didn't go into a forest like you did in-- in some other areas.
Holland had no big forests anywhere.
You went to live in somebody's attic or somebody's cellar, and you couldn't move.
You were dependent on them.
And usually it wasn't in the same-- usually, it was not in the same village where you lived, because it was much too dangerous.
Because you know, there were some very-- even for the Dutch were not good to the Jews.
They should have been better, because most of the Jews were living with them for many generations.
And they were very liberal, the Dutch, but during the war, they forgot about that.
You know.
But there were some very fine people who at very great risk to themselves hid people.
You know.
It's-- it's a very complicated thing to hide somebody.
Not only do you have them living in your house, you have to supply them with food and food was rationed.
And once you're not-- you don't exist anymore.
You didn't get a ration card, so they had to buy the food on the black market, which was very, very difficult.
You know, it was very dangerous too.
Now if you had one or two people hiding, that mean you had to feed one or two people more.
And you had to have money, too, for that also, to be able to sustain.
Because most of these families didn't have-- were not wealthy enough that they could hide you and--and also support you.
And of course, the risk was that if you were caught, you-- not only were you done for but the family who hid you were also shot or taken to camps.
So it was a very dangerous and very brave thing to do.
You know, it wasn't the Germans who found them, it was somebody who went to the Germans and said, hey, I think there are some Jews living at Mr.
So-and-so's or my neighbors or whatever.
You couldn't trust your neighbors.
This was the-- the terrible part about it.
To Vught went my sister, Edith, my Uncle Max, my Aunt Paula, my cousin, Alfred, and that's it.
And-- three, four, five of us.
Yeah.
Did I mention everybody?
Yeah.
Ruth, my cousin Ruth.
Did I mention her?
The oldest-- she was the oldest.
We all went together.
The five of us went together.
We could-- of course, we took-- took our clothes with us.
You could take at least one suitcase with you.
And when we got to Vught, it was like a shock.
You know.
Even so, my sister was quite happy in a way.
You know.
She was happy to get out of Dinxperlo because of these nightly bombardments had gotten on her nerves.
You knwo.
Was-- it wasn't-- wasn't very pleasant.
She was always afraid that one of the nights a bomb would fall on us, you know, which could easily happen.
And it was an important factory, and it was important to the Germans.
There are people to this very day who say we should boycott them because they used slave labor, and they made a profit.
But even so, they claim that they had to do it because the Germans made them do it.
That's neither here nor there anyways.
That's not part of my story.
Vught was-- men were put in one side, and women were put in another part of the camp.
I was together with my cousin but not with my sister.
My sister was together with my aunt and my other cousin for a while.
And my uncle was in a different part, OK?
What happened in Vught was it was divided according to whatever you were.
If you were Jewish, you had a yellow star.
But there were not only Jews there.
There were, for instance, if you were a Communist, and you were caught-- supposedly were a Communist-- you had a red triangle on you-- on your suit, or some of them wore the striped suits already.
But in Vught, mostly they didn't.
Usually you wore your own clothes, whatever you had.
And then if you were supposedly a black marketeer, you had a-- a black little triangle.

And then if you were somebody they didn't like, or they called them homosexual, but they necessarily-- weren't necessarily homosexual, but they made them put a pink triangle on, and so on and so forth.
And-- and there were not only Jews there, there were other people there too.
And every morning, there was roll call, but you had to be there, too.
And there were people trying-- there, people tried to escape.
Not the Jews mainly.
I think mainly they were the non-Jews, the-- the people that would work in the underground what got caught and-- and they had connections.
If they were caught, they were shot usually at during the roll call, you know, which was, I guess, a scary sight.
I don't think-- I don't think they were hung, I think they were mostly shot.
I don't remember anybody being hung.
But basically, it was a very Spartan existence.
We lived in a big barracks.
They didn't know quite what to do with us, because we were kids, a lot of kids.
They use-- they used to sort of chase us all over the place.
I don't know, from one end to the other.
I don't know quite what the purpose behind it was, because they couldn't really send us out to-- to work.
You know, we were too young for that.
And I think they wanted to get it over as soon as possible.
And I think-- I don't think they knew themselves how-- why we were there.
You know.
And after about six weeks in this camp, we were all called to be put on the train to Westerbork.
We were together, actually, we were called together.
My cousin, myself, my sister, my uncle, and my aunt, and-- and-- not my-- not my older cousin, Ruth.
She-- she was working in the Phillips works, so she remained in Vught.
But the rest of us, Alfred, myself, and my Uncle Max, and my Aunt Paula were sent to Westerbork.
I would say it was in early spring, probably.
You know.
When we got to Westerbork, it was on a Monday.
Why do I know it was a Monday?
Because the next day-- every Tuesday, there were trains to-- they're the transport to east to Auschwitz, or to Sobibor, which I found out later.
And we arrived on a Monday evening, and my cousin and myself were separated from the rest of the family, put up in a different barracks.
I don't know if-- if my sister was with my aunt or not.
I don't know, but I-- she wasn't with me.
And during the night from this particular barracks, they were called-- people were called to go on the transport the next day.
And our name was called also, myself and-- and Alfred's name, very clearly.
I know, Fred Spiegel called.
That was my name.
Alfred Spiegel.
You know.
And most of the people in the barracks were called, and most of them-- that's what they often did.
They-- you came from one place, and they right of way tried to get rid of you to the-- to-- to Auschwitz.
Because in Westerbork, people always tried to get to deferment.
And once you established yourself, you managed often to get a longer deferment.
Anyway, we were called, and I know that my uncle had always been afraid.
He had heard of transfers east to Poland, and he had been on the Russian front in the First World War.
And he knew what it-- what it was all about.
He was very much afraid, you know, because he knew that Poland was-- aside from the Germans being there, it was known for its anti-Semitism so he knew it wasn't a good place to go to.
And I know they told you work camps and all that, but--
So we were called in early dawn, and we were walking.
You know.
We were sort of isolated.
During the days-- in the day when the transport left, you were not allowed to speak.
Nobody was allowed to speak to you, you're not allowed to speak to anybody.
Nobody was allowed to go out of the barracks.
You were confined to the barracks with people who didn't go on the transport.
You walked along certain paths guarded by Dutch police and German SS with dogs, and also with Jewish police, camp police, OK?
And we were walking along there, and I was very scared because, well, first of what my uncle had said.
Number two, everybody else was scared so it didn't-- you know, all the adults were scared so I was scared too.
Number three, I didn't see anybody who I knew.
I didn't see my sister, didn't see my uncle and aunt.
I just was with my cousin who was a year older and this scared me.
I said to myself, you know, I don't want to go anywhere on my own.
So when we got to the train, which was-- it wasn't the regular train.
It was one of those cattle wagons with straw on the floor.
They put 80 or whatever people on the-- on the wagon.
I screamed very loudly, and my cousin screamed because I started to scream.
And a lot of people were wailing and crying, so it wasn't anything special, but maybe, could be that I screamed louder.
But I know some German guard went to a Dutch policeman, asked what the-- what the commotion was all about.
So the Dutch policeman said, I-- I guess he doesn't want to go on this train.
You know.
So the-- the German policeman said to [? the guard, ?] then take him off immediately.
It's too much commotion, you know, and I don't like it.
So I was taken off the train together with my cousin and put aside, you know, till the train left.
We were kept in sort of a holding place.
There were some other people taken off the train, too.
I wasn't the only one, but they were taken for different reasons.

They were taken off because at the last moment, the commandants decided that their deferment was still good and they shouldn't go, or they had good connections with-- with-- with the Jewish commandant-- there was a Jewish commandant, too.
And maybe he knew them and tried to get them off.
So a few other people were taken off because they were privileged.
But I was taken off with my cousin because I'd screamed-- for no other reason.
Of course, this-- I found out years later, this particular train went to Sobibor and nobody survived.
Nobody came back.
Very few Jews survived Sobibor.
You know that.
Sobibor was a death camp.
There was no selection like in Auschwitz, there was just-- this was just a death camp, and-- and a majority of people were taken straight to the gas chamber.
And-- and there were no known survivors of this particular transport.
So if I wouldn't have screamed, I wouldn't be sitting here today.
You know.
That's-- I always tell this to people, and that's the strange quirks of life sometimes.
You know, the little things make, you know, make big differences in your life, and this was one of the little things, to scream, and it made all the difference.
You know.
